macro_events: WAR PREMIUM DEFLATION PAUSES — the market is basing just above its ~3-month low for a second session. Westmetall's last official (16-Jun) put cash at $3,359.00 (−1.7%) and 3M at $3,389.50; on 17-Jun the 3M closed +0.84% at ~$3,422 (cash ~$3,400) and 18-Jun is holding near $3,400 ahead of Friday's (19-Jun) formal signing. The structural-deficit floor is reasserting.

Deal timeline: the US and Iran electronically signed an interim framework on 15-Jun (Trump/Vance; Iran's Speaker Ghalibaf); the formal signing is set for Fri 19-Jun (Switzerland). President Trump has declared the Strait of Hormuz 'reopened,' but the shipping industry remains cautious — mine, drone and missile threats keep owners wary of resuming Gulf calls. Relief is imminent but not yet fully physically live.

Physical reality check: the deal restores the Gulf logistics path but not the damaged capacity. Middle East ~9–10% of global supply (~25% of ex-China) with ~3–3.2 Mt of capacity crippled; EGA's Al Taweelah needs ~12 mo to restore and ALBA remains partially suspended. A 3–6 mo lag separates the diplomatic resolution from actual metal — the structural deficit persists.

Macro turned a headwind: the June FOMC (concluded ~17-Jun) signaled growing support for rate hikes on sticky inflation — gold fell ~2% and the USD held firm (DXY ~99.5). Cross-asset confirmation from oil: Brent based at ~$78.6 (lowest since early March, ~40% off the conflict peak) as the Hormuz reopening deflates the energy risk premium — disinflationary and supportive of smelting economics, but reinforcing the bearish-premium tone.

Ex-China supply pressure intact: China unwrought + semis exports ran +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May (Fastmarkets sees 2026 China exports +12–18%, potentially ~7.2 Mt) and Indonesian smelter output is rising; weak China data adds demand worry. The cash–3M curve has narrowed (cash ~$22 below 3M) but remains in contango.
lme_commentary: Commentary: The war-premium unwind has paused. Westmetall's last official (16-Jun) marked the ~3-month low — cash $3,359.00 (−1.7%), 3M $3,389.50 — down from the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). On 17-Jun the 3M rebounded +0.84% to ~$3,422 (cash ~$3,400) and 18-Jun is holding near $3,400 as the market bases ahead of Friday's signing. The cash–3M curve has narrowed from a 19-yr-high backwardation (~$69 on 08-Jun) to a contango (cash ~$22 below 3M), confirming acute front-end scarcity has eased while total stocks stay low. The structural floor remains real: inventories ~316–318kt (−37% YTD from ~509kt in Jan, ~1.4 days of global output, ~92% Russian-origin), EGA ~12 mo to restore, ALBA partially suspended, and a 3–6 mo lag before metal physically returns. Net: a lower, more volatile but supported price. Next catalysts — the 19-Jun signing and the actual Strait reopening (shippers still cautious), plus the USD/Fed path after the hawkish June FOMC.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$355–360/t FOB East Australia; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; easing on the building surplus

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$357/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer Platts API region (~$306/t; FOB futures ~$309); a ~1.2 Mt 2026 surplus is building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.

Hormuz reopening (post the 19-Jun signing) reverses the rerouting premium — alumina cargo flows through the Gulf should normalize over coming weeks, easing the logistics distortion that had benefited China.
Bauxite: Guinea's stricter export controls still tighten raw-material supply and add upstream cost pressure — a structural offset independent of the Gulf truce.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply disruption that lifted regional refining costs should ease as the blockade lifts; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery may restart ahead of the smelter.
Net: input-cost picture skews softer post-deal (alumina ample, energy/logistics relief), though Guinea bauxite controls keep a floor under raw-material risk.
net_read: REGIME SHIFT INTACT — WAR-PREMIUM UNWIND PAUSES, PHYSICAL DEFICIT PERSISTS. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock; the US-Iran interim deal (electronically signed 15-Jun, formal signing 19-Jun in Switzerland) has unwound much of that premium. The market is now basing: Westmetall's last official (16-Jun) marked the ~3-month low (cash $3,359.00, −1.7%), 17-Jun closed +0.84% (3M ~$3,422; cash ~$3,400), and 18-Jun is holding near $3,400 ahead of tomorrow's signing. The cash–3M curve has narrowed to a ~$22 contango. Both desks still see the supply gap persisting: BofA flags near-term downside (China output, Middle East inventory releases, Indonesian supply) while JPMorgan stresses invisible inventories must deplete before visible LME stocks draw. EGA needs ~12 mo to restore, ALBA is partially suspended, the Strait physically reopens only as the signing is confirmed (shippers still cautious on mine/drone/missile risk), LME stocks keep drawing (~316–318kt, ~1.4 days, ~92% Russian-origin), oil is basing at a ~3-mo low (~$78.6 Brent), and a firm USD after the hawkish June FOMC caps rallies. Expect a lower, more volatile but supported price. Watch: the 19-Jun signing and the actual Strait reopening, EGA/ALBA restart timing, China export pace (Fastmarkets +12–18%), and the USD/Fed path.

feed.text: Aluminium is holding near $3,400 (just above the ~2-month/~3-month low) for a second session as the market awaits Friday's (19-Jun) formal US-Iran signing; the structural-deficit floor (EGA ~12 mo, ALBA partially suspended, LME ~316–318kt) caps downside.
LME 3M closed +0.84% at ~$3,422 (cash ~$3,400) — a rebound off the 16-Jun official ~3-mo low; the cash–3M curve narrowed to a ~$22 contango as the front end firmed.
June FOMC signaled growing support for rate hikes on sticky inflation — gold fell ~2% and the USD held firm (DXY ~99.5), a headwind for dollar-priced metals.

President Trump declared the Strait of Hormuz 'reopened' after the 15-Jun electronic signing, but the shipping industry stays cautious (mine/drone/missile risk) ahead of the 19-Jun formal signing — logistics relief imminent, not yet fully live.

Oil based at a ~3-month low — Brent ~$78.6 (lowest since early March, ~40% off the conflict peak), WTI ~$76.6 — as the Hormuz reopening deflates the energy risk premium. Disinflationary, supportive of smelter costs.
Westmetall confirmed the 16-Jun official cash at $3,359.00 (−1.7%) and 3M at $3,389.50 (−1.2%) — the ~3-month low that anchored the war-premium unwind.

BofA (Widmer): aluminium 'vulnerable near-term' as supply risks ease — rising China output, potential Middle East inventory releases and new Indonesian supply compound the downside. JPMorgan (Shearer): expect an energy-correlated knee-jerk, but the 'major supply gap' remains until invisible inventories deplete.
LME inventory drew to ~316–318kt (~92% Russian-origin), down ~37% YTD; available units stay scarce — the deficit is unaffected by the deal.

news.headline: Aluminium bases near $3,400 for a second session: 16-Jun official cash $3,359.00 (the ~3-mo low), 17-Jun 3M +0.84% to ~$3,422, 18-Jun holding ahead of the 19-Jun signing.

US-Iran framework electronically signed 15-Jun; formal signing 19-Jun (Switzerland). Trump declares Hormuz 'reopened,' but shippers stay cautious on mine/drone/missile risk; ceasefire extended 60 days.
news.source: CNN / NPR / The Hill / CNBC (15–17 Jun)

news.headline: Deal restores Gulf logistics but not capacity: Middle East ~9–10% of global supply (~25% ex-China), ~3–3.2 Mt crippled. EGA ~12 mo to restore Al Taweelah; ALBA partially suspended — structural deficit intact.
June FOMC signaled support for rate hikes on sticky inflation — gold −2%, USD firm (DXY ~99.5). A fresh headwind for dollar-priced metals layered on the premium unwind.
Cash–3M curve narrowed from a 19-yr-high backwardation (~$69 on 08-Jun) to a ~$22 contango — acute front-end scarcity easing, though total stocks stay low (~316–318kt).
Oil based at a ~3-mo low (Brent ~$78.6, ~40% off the conflict peak) as the Hormuz reopening deflates the risk premium; disinflationary, supportive of smelter costs.
LME stocks ~316–318kt (−37% YTD), ~1.4 days of global output; ~92% Russian-origin. Deficit unaffected by the deal; visible draws yet to begin in earnest.
China unwrought Al + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May; Fastmarkets sees 2026 exports +12–18% (~7.2 Mt); BofA flags rising Indonesian output; weak China data dents demand.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 18-Jun (no new releases since the prior run). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa confirmed 22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (no guidance policy — has hedged ~67% of 2026 primary output at ~$3,000/t and ~51% of premiums at ~$571/t, net realized premium guided $530–580/t). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com, Zawya/TradeArabia, GuruFocus.

commercial.text: The two-day base near $3,400 (after the 16-Jun ~3-mo low at $3,359) shows the war premium unwinding in an orderly fashion — but unrepaired Gulf capacity (EGA ~12 mo) and low LME stocks cap downside. Accelerate near-dated realization and ensure LME hedges are in place on priced tonnes; do NOT chase a top that has already broken, and do NOT over-hedge forward at distressed levels given the intact structural deficit. Maximise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while elevated.

The Gulf outage drove MJP Q2 +80% q/q (~$351.5) and record US MW premiums; Q3 negotiations are under downward pressure (~$250–330) as the Hormuz reopening (post 19-Jun signing) normalizes support — CIF Japan and EDU are already easing. Lock in elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW, ahead of the 3–6 mo physical-tightness lag giving way to normalized flows. As a non-disrupted supplier, capture premium share while EGA/ALBA remain impaired.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$357/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer API region (~$306/t; Platts FOB futures ~$309); the deal reverses the Hormuz rerouting premium. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing on remaining strength; if buyer for smelting, exploit softer spot windows as Gulf flows normalize.

Hormuz reopening and blockade removal should lower Gulf freight, war-risk insurance and routing risk — but the Strait physically reopens only as the 19-Jun signing is confirmed and shippers remain cautious (mine/drone/missile risk), so relief is not yet fully live. Keep contingency routing and demurrage clauses live through the 60-day window until normalization is confirmed.

Manage the transition narrative with strategic accounts: physical tightness persists through the lag even as the geopolitical risk recedes and headline prices fall. Protect OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and convert reliability through the volatility into relationship equity and premium tolerance.

Track: (1) the durability of the base near $3,400 and any premium-unwind re-acceleration on signing; (2) deal implementation — 19-Jun signing, the actual Strait reopening, shipowner safe-passage confirmation, 60-day window durability; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path after the hawkish June FOMC; (5) China export pace (Fastmarkets +12–18%), Indonesian ramp & Section 232; (6) Guinea bauxite / alumina balance.

bottom_line: A regime shift that is holding, not collapsing: after the 16-Jun ~3-month low (official cash $3,359.00, −1.7%), the market has based near $3,400 for two sessions (17-Jun 3M +0.84% to ~$3,422) as it awaits Friday's (19-Jun) formal US-Iran signing and the Strait reopening. The war premium is deflating in an orderly way — but unrepaired Gulf capacity, drawing LME stocks (~316–318kt) and a 3–6 month supply lag keep a structural floor, and both BofA and JPMorgan agree the supply gap persists even as near-term risks skew down (now compounded by a hawkish June FOMC). For a reliable Gulf-adjacent producer: lock in elevated premiums and near-dated realization before flows normalize, hedge priced tonnes through the volatility, and avoid over-committing forward at distressed levels.

Trading Economics; Discovery Alert (17–18 Jun-26: 3M ~$3,422, +0.84%; holding near $3,400)
CNN / NPR / The Hill / CNBC / PBS (15-Jun electronic signing; formal signing 19-Jun; 60-day ceasefire; shippers cautious)
Bank of America (Michael Widmer) & JPMorgan (Gregory Shearer) via Bloomberg/Mining.com
Trading Economics / Federal Reserve (gold −2%, USD firm, 17-Jun)
Platts FOB Australia futures (~$309) & SMM/metal.com / Fastmarkets MB-ALU-0002
S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets (Q1 MJP $195; Q2 ~$351.5; US MW structurally elevated on 50% Section 232)
GAC China via SMM/ChemAnalyst; Fastmarkets; metal.com (SHFE ~CNY 24,230/t)
caveats: The US-Iran deal was electronically signed 15-Jun; the formal MOU signing is set for 19-Jun. Trump has declared the Strait of Hormuz reopened, but shippers remain cautious on safe passage (mine/drone/missile risk) and the ceasefire is extended 60 days — logistics relief is imminent but not yet fully physically live, and damaged Gulf capacity is unrepaired.

Westmetall's latest published official is 16-Jun (cash $3,359.00, 3M $3,389.50, stock 318,000 t); the 17-Jun official was not yet posted at run time. The 17-Jun cash (~$3,400) is a live/CFD-derived estimate from the confirmed 17-Jun 3M close (~$3,422) and the narrowing contango — reconcile to Westmetall once the official prints.

Macro/FX/energy values are the latest available reads and are point-in-time; oil (Brent ~$78.6 / WTI ~$76.6), DXY (~99.5) and gold (~$4,280) reflect 17–18 Jun and specific levels should be re-validated against live feeds before use.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are most recent public anchors / status for the 2026 war-elevated regime — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.
SHFE front-month is a last-reported reference (~CNY 24,230/t SMM spot, excl-VAT USD equivalent); verify on SMM / SHFE for the live print.
Alumina indices diverge by methodology (Fastmarkets/SMM FOB Australia vs Platts API region) — use the index contractually relevant to the trade.
This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

ai_analysis: What changed: the war-premium unwind has paused. After the 16-Jun official ~3-mo low (cash $3,359.00, −1.7%), the 3M rebounded +0.84% on 17-Jun to ~$3,422 (cash ~$3,400) and 18-Jun is holding — the market bases the day before Friday's signing.

Term structure: the cash–3M curve narrowed from a 19-yr-high backwardation (~$69 on 08-Jun) to a ~$22 contango — front-end scarcity easing, even as total LME stocks keep drawing (~316–318kt, −37% YTD).

Macro turned a headwind: the June FOMC's hawkish lean pushed gold −2% and kept the USD firm (DXY ~99.5); oil based at a ~3-mo low (Brent ~$78.6) — disinflationary but reinforcing the bearish-premium tone.

Sentiment skew: news flow stays net-bearish near-term (premium unwind, oil at 3-mo lows, hawkish Fed, China +12–18% export ramp, BofA caution) — but the structural floor (JPM 'major supply gap') is limiting downside as the price bases.

Forward bias unchanged: base case (50%) = a lower, choppier but supported price ($3,300–3,600 for 2026). Near-term watch is binary on the 19-Jun signing and the actual Strait reopening; main upside risk = a slipped EGA/ALBA restart, main downside = fast Gulf normalization plus China/Indonesia supply.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): MJP Q2 settled near a 10-yr high (~$351/t, +80% q/q); CIF Japan spot easing (~$368) as Hormuz transit normalizes. Q3 under downward pressure (~$250–330).
European (EDU): ~$494/t, easing vs last month (~$519 avg) — demand-led and softening.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,555/t (easing); duty-paid P1020A ~$2,174/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff, the slowest to normalize.
Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf supply outage and are now rolling over; the 19-Jun signing and the Strait reopening are the key downside catalysts into H2-26.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$59–62 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$32–34); −~50% since Jan-25 on oversupply — Guinea weighing export curbs (SMM/Fastmarkets)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$360/t) easing on Q2 oversupply; API region softer (~$307; FOB futures ~$309). Surplus building into H2.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$60/dmt (−~50% YoY) — but Guinea's export-curb threat is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite but caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q1-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (cash $13,682/t, ~$6.21/lb, eased; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; ~2,653 (17-Jun, −0.64%), a >2-mo low; Capesize BCI ~3,877 — Trading Economics / HandyBulk / Bloomberg

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz; Trump declares it reopened but shippers stay cautious (mine/drone/missile risk) — relief imminent

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: Spiked during the closure; should unwind post-signing — keep cover live through the 60-day ceasefire

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround during closure — costly contingency; relief as Hormuz normalizes

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing normalises

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,980/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics is the swing factor now: the Hormuz reopening should unwind freight and war-risk surcharges over coming weeks — but keep contingency routing and cover until normalisation is confirmed; BDI at a 2026-cycle low eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

exec_summary: Aluminum's de-escalation sell-off accelerated. LME cash settled at $3,379.50/t on Monday 15 June, down 4.4% on the day to a two-month low (the lowest since 27 March) and now roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855 — the single worst performer across the base-metals complex (copper +0.3%, tin +2.9%). A parallel ~4% drop in Brent (toward ~$83/bbl) as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated compounded the bearish signal for the most energy-intensive base metal, with a firmer dollar (DXY near 100) a secondary headwind. Even after the correction, aluminum holds about +20% year-to-date and ~+45% year-on-year.

The driver is the US-Iran framework. Trump and Vice President Vance digitally signed a memorandum of understanding for a 60-day all-fronts ceasefire that lifts the US naval blockade and reopens the Strait of Hormuz “permanently toll-free,” with formal signing in Geneva on Friday 19 June and ships reportedly beginning to move. The prompt-scarcity premium is collapsing — the cash-to-3M backwardation has deflated from +$69/t (8 June, a ~19-year high) toward flat. Physical tightness has not yet released, however: LME stocks sit at multi-year lows (~320 kt, ~1.4 days of global output) and are still drawing, the Gulf restart is slow (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, regional output -35% YoY in April), and invisible inventories deplete before visible tightness eases. Net read: the war-risk premium is unwinding fast, but the deal's 60-day conditionality and a months-long physical restart leave a two-sided market — near-term bearish, with sharp-rebound risk if the framework slips.

price_table.note: Latest 15 Jun; 2-mo low (lowest since 27 Mar); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
Tracks cash; 15 Jun est. (official pending) - curve flattening toward contango
War-risk backwardation deflated from +$69 (8 Jun, ~19-yr high); contango risk as scarcity eases
12 Jun official; multi-yr lows ~1.4 days output; still drawing - physical tightness persists
Q2 oversupply persists; oil-led energy relief adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (decade high); Q3 talks open >=$350 but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran MOU signed (digitally by Trump and VP Vance) for a 60-day all-fronts ceasefire - lifts the US naval blockade and reopens the Strait of Hormuz “permanently toll-free,” with formal signing in Geneva on Friday 19 June and ships reportedly beginning to move. This de-escalation is the dominant catalyst unwinding the conflict premium.

Aluminum was the worst base-metals performer on 15 June (-4.4% to $3,379.50, a 2-month low) versus copper +0.3% and tin +2.9% - reflecting its concentrated Hormuz and energy exposure; the metal is now ~12% off the 2 June four-year high of $3,855.

Energy de-risking compounds the bear case: Brent fell ~4% (toward ~$83/bbl) as the war premium deflated; lower power and gas costs pull down the smelter cost floor - directly bearish for the most electricity-intensive (13-15 MWh/t) base metal.

Physical market still tight: LME stocks at multi-year lows (~320 kt, ~1.4 days output) and still drawing; Gulf recovery is slow (EGA ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline, regional output -35% YoY in April), and invisible inventories deplete before visible tightness eases (Shearer/JPM) - risk of premature price optimism.

Compounding supply: China May exports rose to 632 kt but output nears the 45 Mt cap (a finite buffer - BofA/Widmer), Indonesian smelter ramp adds new tonnes, and Guinea bauxite controls are a modest upstream offset. The 60-day conditionality and shipowner caution are the swing factors on how fast Gulf supply actually returns.

implications.text: the de-escalation has flipped the near-term tape decisively lower and the curve is flattening toward contango, so the urgency to sell prompt-only is gone and a window is opening to layer forward hedges on priced tonnes - but avoid chasing forward fixed-price sales into a falling, headline-driven market. Protect value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA) and keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the framework slip on the 60-day conditionality.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 ~$351.5/t, a decade high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) and Q3 MJP talks are opening at or above $350/t, but a confirmed Hormuz reopening is now the principal downside risk to premiums into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 settlements and renewal terms before normalisation feeds through.

softer ex-China alumina (~$300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; a reopened Strait lowers freight and war-risk insurance - begin unwinding contingency-routing premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure, but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe Hormuz passage (caution explicitly flagged).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the 19 June Geneva signing, the credibility and mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit confirmations, EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: 19 June Geneva signing - whether it holds and how the 60-day ceasefire and any renewal mechanism are framed; slippage or collapse is the primary upside-price risk.
Hormuz transit confirmations - whether shipowners validate sustained safe passage; physical supply returns in months, not weeks, with damaged-smelter restarts gating the pace.
LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve - continued draws and any re-steepening would signal the deficit persists despite the peace narrative, while a flip to contango confirms the unwind.
Compounding bears versus structural deficit - China output against the 45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, the visible/invisible inventory gap, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the USD.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (through 12 June); 15 June LME settlement (-4.4% to $3,379.50, 2-month low) via New Straits Times/Reuters and Discovery Alert; benchmark price, USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran framework / MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening and 19 June Geneva signing via CNN, NBC News, NPR, ABC News and Al Jazeera (14-15 June); inventory and scenario commentary citing JPMorgan (Shearer) and Bank of America (Widmer) via Discovery Alert; China May export data via prior GAC customs reporting; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts; regional premiums (MJP/CIF Japan, US Midwest) via S&P Global Platts and Fastmarkets.
weekly.caveat: Note: 15 June 3-month, SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
